text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic myomectomy, opening the retroperitoneum between the round ligament and infundibulopelvic ligament. The uterine artery is identified crossing from lateral to medial, and the ureter is pushed down to safely coagulate the artery. The same dissection is performed on the opposite side, with the uterine artery clearly visible at its origin.

The surgeon makes a large uterine incision at the most prominent part of the fibroid, which measures approximately fifteen to twenty centimeters. The fibroid is enucleated by extending the incision, revealing its deep extension into the submucosal plane. Traction and counter traction are applied to remove the fibroid from the uterus, taking care not to damage the endometrial cavity.

The surgeon sutures the large uterine incision, ensuring all dead space is approximated to prevent hematoma formation. This technique minimizes fibrosis and promotes strong tissue healing, preserving uterine integrity for future fertility.

One must also keep in mind that the uterine artery clipping which has to be performed does not affect the fertility in any way. However, it greatly reduces the chances of recurrence of these fibroids. Typically, in such a large case, otherwise vasopressin would have been used. However, when we try and avoid vasopressin, the anesthetist is the happiest person because all the complications related to vasopressin which includes peripheral vasoconstriction, other cardiac complications can be absolutely avoided. in all these cases.

The surgeon performs multilayer suturing on the uterine incision, demonstrating complex needle manipulation at various angles. The reduced external blood loss is visible due to prior uterine artery coagulation. The suturing technique includes inverted baseball sutures, followed by a roder's round knot to prevent suture slippage. The large fibroid is removed from the cavity using morcellation, leaving a healthy wound with minimal blood loss.
